original_article:
BERLIN (Financial Times) — Escalating geopolitical tensions between the European Union and Russia over energy policy have triggered a cascading crisis in Germany's automotive industry, with major manufacturers warning of sustained production disruptions.

Mercedes-Benz Group AG announced that it would temporarily reduce shifts at its Stuttgart and Bremen assembly plants after critical component supplies were disrupted by new trade restrictions targeting Russian palladium and nickel exports. Germany sources approximately 40% of its palladium—essential for catalytic converters—from Russian suppliers.

"The geopolitical environment has created unprecedented uncertainty for European automotive manufacturers," said Ola Källenius, CEO of Mercedes-Benz. "We are working urgently to diversify our raw material supply chains, but this will take time."

The German Association of the Automotive Industry (VDA) warned that the disruptions could reduce national vehicle output by up to 15% this quarter. The crisis has been compounded by rising energy costs, with industrial electricity prices in Germany now three times higher than pre-crisis levels.

Shares in major German automotive suppliers fell sharply on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange, with Continental AG down 4.2% and ZF Friedrichshafen issuing a profit warning.
paraphrases:
BERLIN — Geopolitical tensions between the European Union and Russia are inflicting serious damage on Germany's automotive industry. Mercedes-Benz Group AG said it would cut shifts at its Stuttgart and Bremen plants after new trade restrictions on Russian palladium and nickel disrupted component supplies.

Germany sources roughly 40% of its palladium—critical for catalytic converter production—from Russian suppliers. Mercedes-Benz CEO Ola Källenius described the geopolitical situation as creating unprecedented uncertainty for European automotive manufacturers.

The VDA, Germany's automotive industry association, warned of a potential 15% drop in national vehicle output this quarter. Rising energy costs have compounded the pressure, with industrial electricity prices now triple their pre-crisis levels. Shares in automotive suppliers Continental AG and ZF Friedrichshafen fell sharply on the Frankfurt exchange.

BERLIN (AP) — Germany's automotive sector is being squeezed by escalating EU-Russia tensions, with Mercedes-Benz Group AG announcing production cuts at two major assembly plants.

The automaker said its Stuttgart and Bremen facilities will operate on reduced shifts after trade restrictions on Russian palladium and nickel exports disrupted supplies of materials essential for catalytic converters and battery components. Germany depends on Russia for approximately 40% of its palladium supply.

Ola Källenius, the Mercedes-Benz chief executive, said the company is urgently working to find alternative material sources. The German Association of the Automotive Industry has warned that national vehicle production could fall by up to 15% this quarter. Industrial energy costs in Germany have tripled, adding further pressure on manufacturers.

Escalating geopolitical friction over EU-Russia energy and trade policy is cascading through Germany's automotive manufacturing sector, prompting production adjustments at major OEMs.

Mercedes-Benz Group AG confirmed shift reductions at its Stuttgart and Bremen assembly plants following disruptions to palladium and nickel supplies caused by new trade restrictions targeting Russian exports. Germany's automotive industry depends heavily on Russian palladium for catalytic converter production, sourcing approximately 40% of its requirements from Russian mines.

The VDA warned that the disruptions, combined with industrial energy prices that have tripled from pre-crisis levels, could cut German vehicle output by 15% this quarter. Continental AG shares dropped 4.2% and ZF Friedrichshafen issued a profit warning as the crisis widened across the supply chain.
